probes.drivingQuestion: How do you turn a big messy comparison between two multi-featured things (like a song and your taste) into one single similarity number, and what does a computer actually do to compute that?
probes.ahaRestated: If you represent each thing as a list of numbers (an arrow), you can measure how similar they are by multiplying matching entries and adding the results, and that sum works like a meter for whether the two arrows point the same way, but it also gets thrown off by how long the arrows are, not just their direction.
probes.retrievalAnswer: Two arrows go in, and you multiply their matching entries together, then add up those products to get one score.

map.note: the 'no single ruler works, waiting for tangles to agree is a lottery' bit was a little abstract on first pass, I wasn't sure what 'agreeing on every quality at once' concretely meant until later.
the tiny numeric example (2,1) and (1,3) making 5 was the first moment it actually clicked what 'multiply then add' means.
probe arrow, four candidates, bars, mono readouts all introduced in one breath, felt like a lot of new visual elements at once.
the frozen-everything-but-the-probe sweep was satisfying, made it obvious the score depends on angle.
no real symbol issue here, but I had to pause and re-derive why a longer arrow raises the score even though direction didn't change; not immediately intuitive.
naming it 'a . b' after already having built the intuition felt earned, that landed well.
the meter dial (agree/ignore/oppose) recap felt a bit redundant with what beat-02 already showed, my attention dipped slightly here.

I predicted the score would just 'drift up' not double exactly, so the reveal was informative, but I had to rewind my own assumption rather than the video explaining why linearity implies exact doubling.
'a loud song wins on sheer loudness whether or not taste agrees' finally made the crack concrete and worth caring about.

the closing question 'what is the cheapest fix that keeps direction and forgets size' assumes I already know the answer is normalization, but the word normalization or dividing by length was never actually said anywhere in the video, so I'm not sure what answer it wants.

advisory.note: engaging and the arrow/meter framing mostly landed, but it was 9:15pm and a couple of stretches (the instrument assembly, the dial recap) asked me to hold a lot of visual state in my head at once.
